sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 38-year-old male, presented with a complaint of shortness of breath, which he describes as a sudden feeling of not being able to catch his breath.
He reports this symptom has been ongoing for some time and is not relieved by rest.
He mentions that he has noticed this symptom in various situations, including at home, outside, and in the presence of his dog.
He denies any other symptoms such as rash, nausea, vomiting, or lip/throat swelling.
He has a history of heavy drinking, which he reports leads to diarrhea.
He denies any issues with allergies in the past and does not have any pets besides a dog.
He has recently moved to a new city and has noticed the symptom since moving.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of heavy drinking, which leads to diarrhea.
He has no known allergies.
Review of systems: The patient reports shortness of breath, which is his main complaint.
Current medications: I prescribed albuterol inhaler as needed for asthma symptoms.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's pulse is normal, and his pulse ox is normal.
Physical exam: The patient appears to be in no distress.
There is no rash, angioedema, or audible stridor.
The lungs exam reveals faint expiratory wheezing bilaterally in all lung fields.
Test Results: The chest X-ray and pulmonary function test were both normal.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has newly diagnosed allergic asthma.
Problem list: Asthma confirmed; Allergic asthma suspected.
Plan:
I prescribed albuterol inhaler as needed for asthma symptoms.
I prescribed Singulair 10mg daily to help decrease the occurrences of asthma symptoms.
I will proceed with allergy testing, starting with skin testing, to identify potential triggers.
The patient will return next week for skin testing.
I instructed the patient to continue taking the albuterol inhaler as soon as symptoms start and to take Singulair daily.
Follow-up: The patient will return next week for skin testing to schedule.
